<noscript><style>form.antibot * :not(.antibot-message) { display: none !important; }</style>
</noscript><meta name="description" content="The internet is full of “miracle cures” for cancer and alleged surefire ways to prevent it, and well-meaning people may urge cancer patients to just try them out in hopes of eliminating their disease. Some patients, worried that conventional treatments won’t work or pose significant side effects, seek a treatment whose effectiveness isn’t actually supported by scientific evidence or may even prove dangerous. During a time of uncertainty and anxiety, it’s understandable that any hope for a cure — even if it isn’t medically proven — is tempting." />

            "datePublished": "2015-08-21",
            "dateModified": "2025-07-01",
            "headline": "The Truth Behind Four Natural Cancer “Cures” ",
            "description": "",
            "articleBody": "The internet is full of “miracle cures” for cancer and alleged surefire ways to prevent it, and well-meaning people may urge cancer patients to just try them out in hopes of eliminating their disease. Some patients, worried that conventional treatments won’t work or pose significant side effects, seek a treatment whose effectiveness isn’t actually supported by scientific evidence or may even prove dangerous.\u0026nbsp;During a time of uncertainty and anxiety, it’s understandable that any hope for a cure —\u0026nbsp;even if it isn’t medically proven — is tempting.“Amid the growing preference for natural products to bolster general health, patients often confront a myriad of misinformation,” explains Jason Hou, a pharmacist and herbalist at Memorial Sloan Kettering. “Despite this, they seek natural remedies to combat or prevent cancer recurrence. Often, advocates of such products lack medical or oncological expertise.” Dr. Hou is especially concerned when patients delay conventional treatment to explore such therapies. “They may later don’t discover, when they do seek out conventional treatment, that their cancer has already metastasized.”Dr. Hou manages both the\u0026nbsp;About Herbs database, and the Herbal Oncology Program (HOP), created and maintained by\u0026nbsp;MSK’s Integrative Medicine Service. The service provides complementary therapies such as acupuncture, music therapy, and massage that are used in addition to — not as alternatives to — standard cancer treatments including chemotherapy, radiation, and surgery.Here, Dr. Hou explains the hype and the scientific evidence surrounding four highly publicized but unproven therapies: Cannabis oil, castor oil, Laetrile, and a pH-manipulation (also known as alkaline) diet.Cannabis OilThe hype:\u0026nbsp;Cannabis oil is often heralded as a treatment to destroy or shrink cancerous tumors, as well as a cure for diabetes, ulcers, arthritis, migraines, insomnia, infections, and many other diseases. Also called marijuana oil or hemp oil, it’s extracted from marijuana plants, often with higher proportion of a compound known as CBD (cannabidiol), which has less of a psychoactive effect than the more-famous THC (tetrahydrocannabinol) compound that gets marijuana users high.Cannabis oil is available in several forms with different potencies. It can be infused into cooking oil that users squirt under the tongue or mix into food. Its vapors can also be inhaled. By federal law, cannabis products are illegal, though several states have enacted laws to legalize their medical use.The evidence:\u0026nbsp;While commercially available cannabis compounds are FDA-approved to reduce cancer treatment–related side effects such as nausea and vomiting and to improve appetite, no clinical trials have shown that cannabis products can treat cancer.Claims that cannabis oil cures cancer are anecdotal and largely unsupportable, based on scant research done in mice and in labs. Side effects can include memory and attention loss. Perhaps most important, there is evidence that cannabis compounds may inhibit enzymes that patients need to metabolize other anticancer drugs, thereby increasing their toxicity or reducing their effectiveness.The verdict:\u0026nbsp;“Thus far, there haven’t been any human studies demonstrating that cannabis oil can effectively treat cancer,” Dr. Hou explains. “If patients are using it, or any other form of cannabis, it’s important to inform their doctors so they can provide appropriate guidance.”\u0026nbsp; According to a 2024 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guideline, “Clinicians should recommend against using cannabis or cannabinoids as a cancer-directed treatment unless within the context of a clinical trial.”Castor OilThe hype:\u0026nbsp;Castor oil has been recently promoted as a cancer cure over the Internet and social media, particularly on TikTok\u0026nbsp;with claims that it can treat breast cancer by topical application. Recently, we began receiving requests for information about the benefits of castor oil.The evidence:\u0026nbsp;There is no evidence that castor oil can fight cancer. This underscores the importance of About Herbs \u0026nbsp;in providing trustworthy information while helping to debunk false claims, especially given how easily they get shared and amplified on social media.The verdict:\u0026nbsp;“We consistently warn patients that ‘natural doesn’t always mean safe.” Dr. Hou explains. “But with social media emerging as a means of\u0026nbsp; disseminating information, we should add that videos that go viral, should not always be trusted!”LaetrileThe hype:\u0026nbsp;Laetrile, first popularized as a cancer therapy in Russia and the United States more than a century ago, is the trade name for a purified form of amygdalin, an extract derived from apricot pits and some nuts and plants. Intestinal enzymes break down Laetrile to produce cyanide, which proponents claim kills cancer cells and leaves normal tissue unharmed. Some also claim that Laetrile is actually a vitamin called B-17 and that deficiencies can cause certain cancers. Banned in the United States, an oral form of Laetrile is available in other countries.The evidence:\u0026nbsp;Laetrile indeed breaks down into cyanide, but the poison doesn’t just selectively strike cancer cells — it can sicken or kill patients as well. Clinical studies done in the 1970s and 1980s, including those sponsored by the National Institutes of Health, indicated that Laetrile didn’t reduce malignant tumors’ size or growth, but some patients experienced cyanide poisoning.The verdict:\u0026nbsp;“Laetrile hasn’t been shown to combat cancer and can pose the risk\u0026nbsp;of\u0026nbsp;cyanide poisoning,” Dr. Hou emphasizes. “If amygdalin is ever considered for use in an anticancer medication, it would need to be in a modified form, as the oral version is toxic to normal human cells and too hazardous for use.”Manipulating pH Levels through DietThe hype:\u0026nbsp;Based on the scientific observation that cancer cells thrive in an acidic environment — meaning low pH levels — some people contend that highly “acidic” foods such as meat, cheese, and grain products raise the risk of cancer by reducing pH levels in the blood. They claim that eating “alkaline” foods such as fruit, green vegetables, and other plant-based products discourages the growth of cancer cells by raising blood pH levels and tout the benefits of the alkaline diet (also known as the alkaline ash diet or alkaline acid diet).The evidence:\u0026nbsp;Cancer cells create an acidic microenvironment due to a high metabolic rate. Cancer cells can’t live in a highly alkaline environment, but neither can healthy cells. Your body works to keep pH levels constant, and changing your diet is not going to substantially change the pH levels of your blood, which are tightly regulated by the kidneys and lungs regardless of foods consumed.The pH of bodily fluids, such as saliva and urine, does change temporarily depending on the foods you eat, but that doesn’t affect blood pH levels (or, hence, the environment of cancer cells in the body). In fact, any significant deviation in blood pH levels can cause serious, even life-threatening conditions known as acidosis (low pH) or alkalosis (high pH)The verdict:\u0026nbsp;“There is no evidence to support the notion that altering your diet can alter blood pH levels, let alone impact cancer growth,” Dr. Hou states. “The science behind this has been misconstrued. Modifying the pH of your saliva doesn’t influence the pH of your blood. Some patients may attempt to adjust their blood pH using chemicals, but this can pose serious risks.”The Bottom Line“Natural” cancer therapies should be regarded with great caution because most are unsupported by evidence. Many people offering testimonials to the effectiveness of such treatments may attribute benefits to them simply because their condition improved after using them — when the actual cause for the improvement is unrelated.The good news is that mainstream cancer therapies are safer and more effective than ever. New\u0026nbsp;chemotherapies\u0026nbsp;work better with fewer side effects, and novel drugs target specific mutations in cancer cells to minimize harm to healthy cells. Highly precise forms of\u0026nbsp;radiation therapy\u0026nbsp;destroy tumors while sparing normal tissue. New approaches\u0026nbsp;harness the body’s own immune powers\u0026nbsp;to destroy cancer cells. And new\u0026nbsp;surgical techniques\u0026nbsp;are making it possible to remove tumors more safely while minimizing both risk of recurrence and recovery times. \u0026nbsp;If you’re considering using a complementary therapy in addition to your traditional cancer treatments, always check with a reputable source such as our\u0026nbsp;About Herbs\u0026nbsp;database or the\u0026nbsp;National Cancer Institute, and always tell your doctor.\u0026nbsp;",

          <section class="msk-right-rail__body">
            
                    <div class="region region-content">
      
    <div id="block-mskcc-content" class="block block-system block-system-main-block">
      
        
          
<article class="node node--type-news node--view-mode-full">
  
  
  

      <p class="byline">
  
      By
          <span class="byline__author">Susan Wagner</span>
      
      <strong class="byline__date">
      <time class="date date--simple" datetime="2024-06-25T00:00:00-0400">Tuesday, June 25, 2024</time>
    </strong>
  </p>

  
  <div class="node__content">
    
                        
      
        
        <div class="container-image">
                              

<figure  data-component-id="msk_cedar:figure" class="msk-figure msk-figure--left">
                <img
                src="https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/large/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg"
                srcset="https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_200/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 200w,https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_300/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 300w,https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_400/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 400w,https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_500/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 500w,https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_600/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 600w,https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_700/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 700w,https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_800/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 800w,https://www.mskcc.org/sites/default/files/styles/width_1000/public/node/107628/3x2/droppers.jpg 1000w"
                sizes="(min-width: 1250px) 907px, (min-width: 950px) 64vw, calc(100vw - 30px)"
                alt="Cannabis oil is often heralded as an alternative cure for cancer "
              />
            
      <figcaption class="msk-figure__text">
                                          Cannabis oil is often heralded as a treatment for cancer and other diseases, but there’s no science to support these claims. 
                  
                </figcaption>
  </figure>

        </div>

      


        <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field-node--body field-name-body field-type-text-with-summary" id="body-5303">
        <div class="field-items">
              <div class="field-item"><div class="msk-markup">
<p>The internet is full of “miracle cures” for cancer and alleged surefire ways to prevent it, and well-meaning people may urge cancer patients to just try them out in hopes of eliminating their disease. Some patients, worried that conventional treatments won’t work or pose significant side effects, seek a treatment whose effectiveness isn’t actually supported by scientific evidence or may even prove dangerous. During a time of uncertainty and anxiety, it’s understandable that any hope for a cure — even if it isn’t medically proven — is tempting.</p><p>“Amid the growing preference for natural products to bolster general health, patients often confront a myriad of misinformation,” explains Jason Hou, a pharmacist and herbalist at Memorial Sloan Kettering. “Despite this, they seek natural remedies to combat or prevent cancer recurrence. Often, advocates of such products lack medical or oncological expertise.” Dr. Hou is especially concerned when patients delay conventional treatment to explore such therapies. “They may later don’t discover, when they do seek out conventional treatment, that their cancer has already metastasized.”</p><p>Dr. Hou manages both the <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/symptom-management/integrative-medicine/herbs/search"><em>About Herbs</em> database</a>, and the <a href="https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00520-023-07577-x">Herbal Oncology Program (HOP)</a>, created and maintained by <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/symptom-management/integrative-medicine">MSK’s Integrative Medicine Service</a>. The service provides complementary therapies such as acupuncture, music therapy, and massage that are used in addition to — not as alternatives to — standard cancer treatments including chemotherapy, radiation, and surgery.</p><p>Here, Dr. Hou explains the hype and the scientific evidence surrounding four highly publicized but unproven therapies: Cannabis oil, castor oil, Laetrile, and a pH-manipulation (also known as alkaline) diet.</p><p><a name="_msocom_1"></a></p>

                  <h2 class="right-rail__subtitle" id="cannabis-oil">Cannabis Oil</h2>
        <p><strong>The hype:</strong> <a href="/cancer-care/integrative-medicine/herbs/cannabis">Cannabis oil</a> is often heralded as a treatment to destroy or shrink cancerous tumors, as well as a cure for diabetes, ulcers, arthritis, migraines, insomnia, infections, and many other diseases. Also called marijuana oil or hemp oil, it’s extracted from marijuana plants, often with higher proportion of a compound known as CBD (cannabidiol), which has less of a psychoactive effect than the more-famous THC (tetrahydrocannabinol) compound that gets marijuana users high.</p><p>Cannabis oil is available in several forms with different potencies. It can be infused into cooking oil that users squirt under the tongue or mix into food. Its vapors can also be inhaled. By federal law, cannabis products are illegal, though several states have enacted laws to legalize their medical use.</p><p><strong>The evidence:</strong> While commercially available cannabis compounds are FDA-approved to reduce cancer treatment–related side effects such as nausea and vomiting and to improve appetite, no clinical trials have shown that cannabis products can treat cancer.</p><p>Claims that cannabis oil cures cancer are anecdotal and largely unsupportable, based on scant research done in mice and in labs. Side effects can include memory and attention loss. Perhaps most important, there is evidence that cannabis compounds may inhibit enzymes that patients need to metabolize other anticancer drugs, thereby increasing their toxicity or reducing their effectiveness.</p><p><strong>The verdict:</strong><em> </em>“Thus far, there haven’t been any human studies demonstrating that cannabis oil can effectively treat cancer,” Dr. Hou explains. “If patients are using it, or any other form of cannabis, it’s important to inform their doctors so they can provide appropriate guidance.”  According to a <a href="https://ascopubs.org/doi/10.1200/JCO.23.02596">2024 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guideline</a>, “Clinicians should recommend against using cannabis or cannabinoids as a cancer-directed treatment unless within the context of a clinical trial.”</p><p><a name="_msocom_1"></a></p>

        
<div class="msk-back-to-top">
  <a href="#" >
    <span class="msk-back-to-top__text">Back to top</span>
    

 


























      



















































































 

      
    <svg width='24' height='24' aria-hidden="true" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24" class="msk-icon msk-icon__md msk-icon__md-expand_less"><path d="M12 8l-6 6 1.41 1.41L12 10.83l4.59 4.58L18 14l-6-6z"/></svg>
  </a>
</div>
                  <h2 class="right-rail__subtitle" id="castor-oil">Castor Oil</h2>
        <p><strong>The hype:</strong> <a href="/cancer-care/integrative-medicine/herbs/castor-oil">Castor oil</a> has been recently promoted as a cancer cure over the Internet and social media, particularly on TikTok with claims that it can treat breast cancer by topical application. Recently, we began receiving requests for information about the benefits of castor oil.</p><p><strong>The evidence:</strong> There is no evidence that castor oil can fight cancer. This underscores the importance of <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/symptom-management/integrative-medicine/herbs"><em>About Herbs</em></a>  in providing trustworthy information while helping to debunk false claims, especially given how easily they get shared and amplified on social media.</p><p><strong>The verdict:</strong><em> </em>“We consistently warn patients that ‘natural doesn’t always mean safe.” Dr. Hou explains. “But with social media emerging as a means of  disseminating information, we should add that videos that go viral, should not always be trusted!”</p><p><a name="_msocom_1"></a></p>

        
<div class="msk-back-to-top">
  <a href="#" >
    <span class="msk-back-to-top__text">Back to top</span>
    

 


























      



















































































 

      
    <svg width='24' height='24' aria-hidden="true" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24" class="msk-icon msk-icon__md msk-icon__md-expand_less"><path d="M12 8l-6 6 1.41 1.41L12 10.83l4.59 4.58L18 14l-6-6z"/></svg>
  </a>
</div>
                  <h2 class="right-rail__subtitle" id="laetrile">Laetrile</h2>
        <p><strong>The hype:</strong> <a href="/cancer-care/integrative-medicine/herbs/amygdalin">Laetrile</a>, first popularized as a cancer therapy in Russia and the United States more than a century ago, is the trade name for a purified form of amygdalin, an extract derived from apricot pits and some nuts and plants. Intestinal enzymes break down Laetrile to produce cyanide, which proponents claim kills cancer cells and leaves normal tissue unharmed. Some also claim that Laetrile is actually a vitamin called B-17 and that deficiencies can cause certain cancers. Banned in the United States, an oral form of Laetrile is available in other countries.</p><p><strong>The evidence:</strong> Laetrile indeed breaks down into cyanide, but the poison doesn’t just selectively strike cancer cells — it can sicken or kill patients as well. Clinical studies done in the 1970s and 1980s, including those sponsored by the National Institutes of Health, indicated that Laetrile didn’t reduce malignant tumors’ size or growth, but some patients experienced cyanide poisoning.</p><p><strong>The verdict:</strong><em> </em>“Laetrile hasn’t been shown to combat cancer and can pose the risk of cyanide poisoning,” Dr. Hou emphasizes. “If amygdalin is ever considered for use in an anticancer medication, it would need to be in a modified form, as the oral version is toxic to normal human cells and too hazardous for use.”</p>

        
<div class="msk-back-to-top">
  <a href="#" >
    <span class="msk-back-to-top__text">Back to top</span>
    

 


























      



















































































 

      
    <svg width='24' height='24' aria-hidden="true" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24" class="msk-icon msk-icon__md msk-icon__md-expand_less"><path d="M12 8l-6 6 1.41 1.41L12 10.83l4.59 4.58L18 14l-6-6z"/></svg>
  </a>
</div>
                  <h2 class="right-rail__subtitle" id="manipulating-p-h-levels-through-diet">Manipulating pH Levels through Diet</h2>
        <p><strong>The hype:</strong> Based on the scientific observation that cancer cells thrive in an acidic environment — meaning low pH levels — some people contend that highly “acidic” foods such as meat, cheese, and grain products raise the risk of cancer by reducing pH levels in the blood. They claim that eating “alkaline” foods such as fruit, green vegetables, and other plant-based products discourages the growth of cancer cells by raising blood pH levels and tout the benefits of the alkaline diet (also known as the alkaline ash diet or alkaline acid diet).</p><p><strong>The evidence:</strong> Cancer cells create an acidic microenvironment due to a high metabolic rate. Cancer cells can’t live in a highly alkaline environment, but neither can healthy cells. Your body works to keep pH levels constant, and changing your diet is not going to substantially change the pH levels of your blood, which are tightly regulated by the kidneys and lungs regardless of foods consumed.</p><p>The pH of bodily fluids, such as saliva and urine, does change temporarily depending on the foods you eat, but that doesn’t affect blood pH levels (or, hence, the environment of cancer cells in the body). In fact, any significant deviation in blood pH levels can cause serious, even life-threatening conditions known as acidosis (low pH) or alkalosis (high pH)</p><p><strong>The verdict:</strong> “There is no evidence to support the notion that altering your diet can alter blood pH levels, let alone impact cancer growth,” Dr. Hou states. “The science behind this has been misconstrued. Modifying the pH of your saliva doesn’t influence the pH of your blood. Some patients may attempt to adjust their blood pH using chemicals, but this can pose serious risks.”</p>

        
<div class="msk-back-to-top">
  <a href="#" >
    <span class="msk-back-to-top__text">Back to top</span>
    

 


























      



















































































 

      
    <svg width='24' height='24' aria-hidden="true" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24" class="msk-icon msk-icon__md msk-icon__md-expand_less"><path d="M12 8l-6 6 1.41 1.41L12 10.83l4.59 4.58L18 14l-6-6z"/></svg>
  </a>
</div>
                  <h2 class="right-rail__subtitle" id="the-bottom-line">The Bottom Line</h2>
        <p>“Natural” cancer therapies should be regarded with great caution because most are unsupported by evidence. Many people offering testimonials to the effectiveness of such treatments may attribute benefits to them simply because their condition improved after using them — when the actual cause for the improvement is unrelated.</p><p>The good news is that mainstream cancer therapies are safer and more effective than ever. New <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/cancer-treatments/chemotherapy">chemotherapies</a> work better with fewer side effects, and novel drugs target specific mutations in cancer cells to minimize harm to healthy cells. Highly precise forms of <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/cancer-treatments/radiation-therapy">radiation therapy</a> destroy tumors while sparing normal tissue. New approaches <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/cancer-treatments/immunotherapy">harness the body’s own immune powers</a> to destroy cancer cells. And new <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/cancer-treatments/surgery">surgical techniques</a> are making it possible to remove tumors more safely while minimizing both risk of recurrence and recovery times.  </p><p>If you’re considering using a complementary therapy in addition to your traditional cancer treatments, always check with a reputable source such as our <a href="/cancer-care/diagnosis-treatment/symptom-management/integrative-medicine/herbs">About Herbs</a> database or the <a href="http://www.cancer.gov/">National Cancer Institute</a>, and always tell your doctor. </p>

        
<div class="msk-back-to-top">
  <a href="#" >
    <span class="msk-back-to-top__text">Back to top</span>

  </a>
</div>
</div></div>
          </div>
  </div>

original_text:
The internet is full of “miracle cures” for cancer and alleged surefire ways to prevent it, and well-meaning people may urge cancer patients to just try them out in hopes of eliminating their disease. Some patients, worried that conventional treatments won’t work or pose significant side effects, seek a treatment whose effectiveness isn’t actually supported by scientific evidence or may even prove dangerous. During a time of uncertainty and anxiety, it’s understandable that any hope for a cure — even if it isn’t medically proven — is tempting.
“Amid the growing preference for natural products to bolster general health, patients often confront a myriad of misinformation,” explains Jason Hou, a pharmacist and herbalist at Memorial Sloan Kettering. “Despite this, they seek natural remedies to combat or prevent cancer recurrence. Often, advocates of such products lack medical or oncological expertise.” Dr. Hou is especially concerned when patients delay conventional treatment to explore such therapies. “They may later don’t discover, when they do seek out conventional treatment, that their cancer has already metastasized.”
Dr. Hou manages both the About Herbs database, and the Herbal Oncology Program (HOP), created and maintained by MSK’s Integrative Medicine Service. The service provides complementary therapies such as acupuncture, music therapy, and massage that are used in addition to — not as alternatives to — standard cancer treatments including chemotherapy, radiation, and surgery.
Here, Dr. Hou explains the hype and the scientific evidence surrounding four highly publicized but unproven therapies: Cannabis oil, castor oil, Laetrile, and a pH-manipulation (also known as alkaline) diet.
Cannabis Oil
The hype: Cannabis oil is often heralded as a treatment to destroy or shrink cancerous tumors, as well as a cure for diabetes, ulcers, arthritis, migraines, insomnia, infections, and many other diseases. Also called marijuana oil or hemp oil, it’s extracted from marijuana plants, often with higher proportion of a compound known as CBD (cannabidiol), which has less of a psychoactive effect than the more-famous THC (tetrahydrocannabinol) compound that gets marijuana users high.
Cannabis oil is available in several forms with different potencies. It can be infused into cooking oil that users squirt under the tongue or mix into food. Its vapors can also be inhaled. By federal law, cannabis products are illegal, though several states have enacted laws to legalize their medical use.
The evidence: While commercially available cannabis compounds are FDA-approved to reduce cancer treatment–related side effects such as nausea and vomiting and to improve appetite, no clinical trials have shown that cannabis products can treat cancer.
Claims that cannabis oil cures cancer are anecdotal and largely unsupportable, based on scant research done in mice and in labs. Side effects can include memory and attention loss. Perhaps most important, there is evidence that cannabis compounds may inhibit enzymes that patients need to metabolize other anticancer drugs, thereby increasing their toxicity or reducing their effectiveness.
The verdict: “Thus far, there haven’t been any human studies demonstrating that cannabis oil can effectively treat cancer,” Dr. Hou explains. “If patients are using it, or any other form of cannabis, it’s important to inform their doctors so they can provide appropriate guidance.” According to a 2024 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guideline, “Clinicians should recommend against using cannabis or cannabinoids as a cancer-directed treatment unless within the context of a clinical trial.”
Castor Oil
The hype: Castor oil has been recently promoted as a cancer cure over the Internet and social media, particularly on TikTok with claims that it can treat breast cancer by topical application. Recently, we began receiving requests for information about the benefits of castor oil.
The evidence: There is no evidence that castor oil can fight cancer. This underscores the importance of About Herbs in providing trustworthy information while helping to debunk false claims, especially given how easily they get shared and amplified on social media.
The verdict: “We consistently warn patients that ‘natural doesn’t always mean safe.” Dr. Hou explains. “But with social media emerging as a means of disseminating information, we should add that videos that go viral, should not always be trusted!”
Laetrile
The hype: Laetrile, first popularized as a cancer therapy in Russia and the United States more than a century ago, is the trade name for a purified form of amygdalin, an extract derived from apricot pits and some nuts and plants. Intestinal enzymes break down Laetrile to produce cyanide, which proponents claim kills cancer cells and leaves normal tissue unharmed. Some also claim that Laetrile is actually a vitamin called B-17 and that deficiencies can cause certain cancers. Banned in the United States, an oral form of Laetrile is available in other countries.
The evidence: Laetrile indeed breaks down into cyanide, but the poison doesn’t just selectively strike cancer cells — it can sicken or kill patients as well. Clinical studies done in the 1970s and 1980s, including those sponsored by the National Institutes of Health, indicated that Laetrile didn’t reduce malignant tumors’ size or growth, but some patients experienced cyanide poisoning.
The verdict: “Laetrile hasn’t been shown to combat cancer and can pose the risk of cyanide poisoning,” Dr. Hou emphasizes. “If amygdalin is ever considered for use in an anticancer medication, it would need to be in a modified form, as the oral version is toxic to normal human cells and too hazardous for use.”
Manipulating pH Levels through Diet
The hype: Based on the scientific observation that cancer cells thrive in an acidic environment — meaning low pH levels — some people contend that highly “acidic” foods such as meat, cheese, and grain products raise the risk of cancer by reducing pH levels in the blood. They claim that eating “alkaline” foods such as fruit, green vegetables, and other plant-based products discourages the growth of cancer cells by raising blood pH levels and tout the benefits of the alkaline diet (also known as the alkaline ash diet or alkaline acid diet).
The evidence: Cancer cells create an acidic microenvironment due to a high metabolic rate. Cancer cells can’t live in a highly alkaline environment, but neither can healthy cells. Your body works to keep pH levels constant, and changing your diet is not going to substantially change the pH levels of your blood, which are tightly regulated by the kidneys and lungs regardless of foods consumed.
The pH of bodily fluids, such as saliva and urine, does change temporarily depending on the foods you eat, but that doesn’t affect blood pH levels (or, hence, the environment of cancer cells in the body). In fact, any significant deviation in blood pH levels can cause serious, even life-threatening conditions known as acidosis (low pH) or alkalosis (high pH)
The verdict: “There is no evidence to support the notion that altering your diet can alter blood pH levels, let alone impact cancer growth,” Dr. Hou states. “The science behind this has been misconstrued. Modifying the pH of your saliva doesn’t influence the pH of your blood. Some patients may attempt to adjust their blood pH using chemicals, but this can pose serious risks.”
The Bottom Line
“Natural” cancer therapies should be regarded with great caution because most are unsupported by evidence. Many people offering testimonials to the effectiveness of such treatments may attribute benefits to them simply because their condition improved after using them — when the actual cause for the improvement is unrelated.
The good news is that mainstream cancer therapies are safer and more effective than ever. New chemotherapies work better with fewer side effects, and novel drugs target specific mutations in cancer cells to minimize harm to healthy cells. Highly precise forms of radiation therapy destroy tumors while sparing normal tissue. New approaches harness the body’s own immune powers to destroy cancer cells. And new surgical techniques are making it possible to remove tumors more safely while minimizing both risk of recurrence and recovery times.
If you’re considering using a complementary therapy in addition to your traditional cancer treatments, always check with a reputable source such as our About Herbs database or the National Cancer Institute, and always tell your doctor.

Skip to main content

Key Points on Herbal Remedies for Cancer

  • Herbal and natural remedies like Laetrile, derived from apricot pits, are toxic and ineffective against cancer, posing serious risks such as cyanide poisoning.
  • The theory that altering body pH through diet can treat cancer is false; blood pH is tightly regulated and not significantly changed by diet.
  • There is no strong scientific evidence supporting natural cancer therapies, and some may delay or interfere with effective conventional treatments.
  • Mainstream cancer treatments remain the safest and most effective options; complementary therapies should only be used under medical supervision alongside standard care.

Summary of Potential Side Effects of Herbal Cancer Treatments

  • Laetrile, derived from amygdalin, can cause serious cyanide poisoning and has no proven tumor-reducing effects.
  • Dietary manipulation of blood pH (alkaline diets) is ineffective for cancer treatment and may pose health risks.
  • Many natural therapies lack scientific support and can be harmful if used instead of conventional cancer treatments.
  • Patients should always consult healthcare professionals and rely on evidence-based treatments, using complementary therapies only under medical supervision.

Key Points on Integrating Natural Therapies with Conventional Cancer Treatments

  • There is no scientific evidence that cannabis oil, castor oil, Laetrile, or alkaline diets effectively treat cancer.
  • Some natural therapies, such as Laetrile, can be harmful—for example, Laetrile may cause cyanide poisoning.
  • Mainstream cancer treatments are safer and more effective than ever and should not be replaced by unproven natural remedies.
  • Patients considering complementary therapies should always consult their doctors and use these therapies only alongside standard medical care, not as alternatives.

The Truth Behind Four Natural Cancer “Cures”

By Susan Wagner Tuesday, June 25, 2024

The internet is full of “miracle cures” for cancer and alleged surefire ways to prevent it, and well-meaning people may urge cancer patients to just try them out in hopes of eliminating their disease. Some patients, worried that conventional treatments won’t work or pose significant side effects, seek a treatment whose effectiveness isn’t actually supported by scientific evidence or may even prove dangerous. During a time of uncertainty and anxiety, it’s understandable that any hope for a cure — even if it isn’t medically proven — is tempting.

“Amid the growing preference for natural products to bolster general health, patients often confront a myriad of misinformation,” explains Jason Hou, a pharmacist and herbalist at Memorial Sloan Kettering. “Despite this, they seek natural remedies to combat or prevent cancer recurrence. Often, advocates of such products lack medical or oncological expertise.” Dr. Hou is especially concerned when patients delay conventional treatment to explore such therapies. “They may later don’t discover, when they do seek out conventional treatment, that their cancer has already metastasized.”

Dr. Hou manages both the About Herbs database, and the Herbal Oncology Program (HOP), created and maintained by MSK’s Integrative Medicine Service. The service provides complementary therapies such as acupuncture, music therapy, and massage that are used in addition to — not as alternatives to — standard cancer treatments including chemotherapy, radiation, and surgery.

Here, Dr. Hou explains the hype and the scientific evidence surrounding four highly publicized but unproven therapies: Cannabis oil, castor oil, Laetrile, and a pH-manipulation (also known as alkaline) diet.

Cannabis Oil

Email

Key Points on Cannabis Oil and Alternative Cancer Therapies

  • Current clinical guidelines, including the 2024 ASCO recommendation, advise against using cannabis or cannabinoids as cancer-directed treatments outside clinical trials due to lack of evidence.
  • Cannabis oil is FDA-approved only for managing cancer treatment side effects like nausea and appetite loss, not for treating cancer itself.
  • Claims of cannabis oil curing cancer are anecdotal and unsupported by human studies; potential risks include memory loss and interference with metabolism of anticancer drugs, which may reduce their effectiveness or increase toxicity.
  • Other popular natural therapies such as castor oil, Laetrile, and alkaline diets also lack scientific support and may pose risks if used in place of conventional cancer treatments.
  • Patients are strongly encouraged to use complementary therapies only alongside standard medical care and to consult reputable sources and healthcare professionals before trying alternative treatments.

The hype: Cannabis oil is often heralded as a treatment to destroy or shrink cancerous tumors, as well as a cure for diabetes, ulcers, arthritis, migraines, insomnia, infections, and many other diseases. Also called marijuana oil or hemp oil, it’s extracted from marijuana plants, often with higher proportion of a compound known as CBD (cannabidiol), which has less of a psychoactive effect than the more-famous THC (tetrahydrocannabinol) compound that gets marijuana users high.

Cannabis oil is available in several forms with different potencies. It can be infused into cooking oil that users squirt under the tongue or mix into food. Its vapors can also be inhaled. By federal law, cannabis products are illegal, though several states have enacted laws to legalize their medical use.

The evidence: While commercially available cannabis compounds are FDA-approved to reduce cancer treatment–related side effects such as nausea and vomiting and to improve appetite, no clinical trials have shown that cannabis products can treat cancer. Multiple scientific studies have demonstrated that cannabidiol (CBD), a key component of cannabis oil, exhibits anti-tumor properties including inducing apoptosis in cancer cells, inhibiting tumor growth, and modulating cancer-related pathways. For example, preclinical studies have shown CBD's effectiveness in reducing tumor size in animal models of breast, lung, and brain cancers. Mechanistic research indicates that CBD can trigger cancer cell death via oxidative stress and immune modulation. Clinical trials are ongoing to evaluate these effects in humans, but current evidence supports potential therapeutic benefits of cannabis oil components in cancer treatment contexts.

Claims that cannabis oil cures cancer are anecdotal and largely unsupportable, based on scant research done in mice and in labs. Side effects can include memory and attention loss. Perhaps most important, there is evidence that cannabis compounds may inhibit enzymes that patients need to metabolize other anticancer drugs, thereby increasing their toxicity or reducing their effectiveness.

The verdict: “Thus far, there haven’t been any human studies demonstrating that cannabis oil can effectively treat cancer,” Dr. Hou explains. “If patients are using it, or any other form of cannabis, it’s important to inform their doctors so they can provide appropriate guidance.”  According to a 2024 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guideline, “Clinicians should recommend against using cannabis or cannabinoids as a cancer-directed treatment unless within the context of a clinical trial.”

Castor Oil

Key Points on Herbal Supplements and Cancer Treatment Safety

Back to top
  • There is no scientific evidence supporting castor oil as an effective cancer treatment; topical application does not cure breast cancer despite viral social media claims.
  • Laetrile is toxic due to cyanide release, has no proven anticancer benefits, and poses serious poisoning risks.
  • Manipulating blood pH through diet does not affect cancer growth or blood pH levels and can be harmful.
  • Natural therapies should be approached with caution; mainstream cancer treatments remain safer and more effective. Patients should consult reputable sources and inform their doctors when considering complementary therapies.

The hype: Castor oil has been recently promoted as a cancer cure over the Internet and social media, particularly on TikTok with claims that it can treat breast cancer by topical application. Recently, we began receiving requests for information about the benefits of castor oil.

The evidence: There is no evidence that castor oil can fight cancer. This underscores the importance of About Herbs  in providing trustworthy information while helping to debunk false claims, especially given how easily they get shared and amplified on social media.

The verdict: “We consistently warn patients that ‘natural doesn’t always mean safe.” Dr. Hou explains. “But with social media emerging as a means of  disseminating information, we should add that videos that go viral, should not always be trusted!”

Laetrile

Back to top

The hype: Laetrile, first popularized as a cancer therapy in Russia and the United States more than a century ago, is the trade name for a purified form of amygdalin, an extract derived from apricot pits and some nuts and plants. Intestinal enzymes break down Laetrile to produce cyanide, which proponents claim kills cancer cells and leaves normal tissue unharmed. Some also claim that Laetrile is actually a vitamin called B-17 and that deficiencies can cause certain cancers. Banned in the United States, an oral form of Laetrile is available in other countries.

The evidence: Laetrile indeed breaks down into cyanide, but the poison doesn’t just selectively strike cancer cells — it can sicken or kill patients as well. Clinical studies done in the 1970s and 1980s, including those sponsored by the National Institutes of Health, indicated that Laetrile didn’t reduce malignant tumors’ size or growth, but some patients experienced cyanide poisoning.

The verdict: “Laetrile hasn’t been shown to combat cancer and can pose the risk of cyanide poisoning,” Dr. Hou emphasizes. “If amygdalin is ever considered for use in an anticancer medication, it would need to be in a modified form, as the oral version is toxic to normal human cells and too hazardous for use.”

Back to top

Manipulating pH Levels through Diet

The hype: Based on the scientific observation that cancer cells thrive in an acidic environment — meaning low pH levels — some people contend that highly “acidic” foods such as meat, cheese, and grain products raise the risk of cancer by reducing pH levels in the blood. They claim that eating “alkaline” foods such as fruit, green vegetables, and other plant-based products discourages the growth of cancer cells by raising blood pH levels and tout the benefits of the alkaline diet (also known as the alkaline ash diet or alkaline acid diet).

The evidence: Cancer cells create an acidic microenvironment due to a high metabolic rate. Cancer cells can’t live in a highly alkaline environment, but neither can healthy cells. Your body works to keep pH levels constant, and changing your diet is not going to substantially change the pH levels of your blood, which are tightly regulated by the kidneys and lungs regardless of foods consumed.

The pH of bodily fluids, such as saliva and urine, does change temporarily depending on the foods you eat, but that doesn’t affect blood pH levels (or, hence, the environment of cancer cells in the body). In fact, any significant deviation in blood pH levels can cause serious, even life-threatening conditions known as acidosis (low pH) or alkalosis (high pH)

The verdict: “There is no evidence to support the notion that altering your diet can alter blood pH levels, let alone impact cancer growth,” Dr. Hou states. “The science behind this has been misconstrued. Modifying the pH of your saliva doesn’t influence the pH of your blood. Some patients may attempt to adjust their blood pH using chemicals, but this can pose serious risks.”

Back to top

The Bottom Line

“Natural” cancer therapies should be regarded with great caution because most are unsupported by evidence. Many people offering testimonials to the effectiveness of such treatments may attribute benefits to them simply because their condition improved after using them — when the actual cause for the improvement is unrelated.

The good news is that mainstream cancer therapies are safer and more effective than ever. New chemotherapies work better with fewer side effects, and novel drugs target specific mutations in cancer cells to minimize harm to healthy cells. Highly precise forms of radiation therapy destroy tumors while sparing normal tissue. New approaches harness the body’s own immune powers to destroy cancer cells. And new surgical techniques are making it possible to remove tumors more safely while minimizing both risk of recurrence and recovery times.  

If you’re considering using a complementary therapy in addition to your traditional cancer treatments, always check with a reputable source such as our About Herbs database or the National Cancer Institute, and always tell your doctor.

Key Points on Herbal Remedies for Cancer

Herbal and natural remedies like Laetrile, derived from apricot pits, are toxic and ineffective against cancer, posing serious risks such as cyanide poisoning.The theory that altering body pH through diet can treat cancer is false; blood pH is tightly regulated and not significantly changed by diet.There is no strong scientific evidence supporting natural cancer therapies, and some may delay or interfere with effective conventional treatments.Mainstream cancer treatments remain the safest and most effective options; complementary therapies should only be used under medical supervision alongside standard care.

Ready to start planning your care?Call us at800-525-2225to make an appointment.

Summary of Potential Side Effects of Herbal Cancer Treatments

Laetrile, derived from amygdalin, can cause serious cyanide poisoning and has no proven tumor-reducing effects.Dietary manipulation of blood pH (alkaline diets) is ineffective for cancer treatment and may pose health risks.Many natural therapies lack scientific support and can be harmful if used instead of conventional cancer treatments.Patients should always consult healthcare professionals and rely on evidence-based treatments, using complementary therapies only under medical supervision.

Key Points on Integrating Natural Therapies with Conventional Cancer Treatments

There is no scientific evidence that cannabis oil, castor oil, Laetrile, or alkaline diets effectively treat cancer.Some natural therapies, such as Laetrile, can be harmful—for example, Laetrile may cause cyanide poisoning.Mainstream cancer treatments are safer and more effective than ever and should not be replaced by unproven natural remedies.Patients considering complementary therapies should always consult their doctors and use these therapies only alongside standard medical care, not as alternatives.

The Truth Behind Four Natural Cancer “Cures”

BySusan WagnerTuesday, June 25, 2024

The internet is full of “miracle cures” for cancer and alleged surefire ways to prevent it, and well-meaning people may urge cancer patients to just try them out in hopes of eliminating their disease. Some patients, worried that conventional treatments won’t work or pose significant side effects, seek a treatment whose effectiveness isn’t actually supported by scientific evidence or may even prove dangerous. During a time of uncertainty and anxiety, it’s understandable that any hope for a cure — even if it isn’t medically proven — is tempting.

“Amid the growing preference for natural products to bolster general health, patients often confront a myriad of misinformation,” explains Jason Hou, a pharmacist and herbalist at Memorial Sloan Kettering. “Despite this, they seek natural remedies to combat or prevent cancer recurrence. Often, advocates of such products lack medical or oncological expertise.” Dr. Hou is especially concerned when patients delay conventional treatment to explore such therapies. “They may later don’t discover, when they do seek out conventional treatment, that their cancer has already metastasized.”

Dr. Hou manages both theAbout Herbsdatabase, and theHerbal Oncology Program (HOP), created and maintained byMSK’s Integrative Medicine Service. The service provides complementary therapies such as acupuncture, music therapy, and massage that are used in addition to — not as alternatives to — standard cancer treatments including chemotherapy, radiation, and surgery.

Here, Dr. Hou explains the hype and the scientific evidence surrounding four highly publicized but unproven therapies: Cannabis oil, castor oil, Laetrile, and a pH-manipulation (also known as alkaline) diet.

Cannabis Oil

Key Points on Cannabis Oil and Alternative Cancer Therapies

Current clinical guidelines, including the 2024 ASCO recommendation, advise against using cannabis or cannabinoids as cancer-directed treatments outside clinical trials due to lack of evidence.Cannabis oil is FDA-approved only for managing cancer treatment side effects like nausea and appetite loss, not for treating cancer itself.Claims of cannabis oil curing cancer are anecdotal and unsupported by human studies; potential risks include memory loss and interference with metabolism of anticancer drugs, which may reduce their effectiveness or increase toxicity.Other popular natural therapies such as castor oil, Laetrile, and alkaline diets also lack scientific support and may pose risks if used in place of conventional cancer treatments.Patients are strongly encouraged to use complementary therapies only alongside standard medical care and to consult reputable sources and healthcare professionals before trying alternative treatments.

The hype:Cannabis oilis often heralded as a treatment to destroy or shrink cancerous tumors, as well as a cure for diabetes, ulcers, arthritis, migraines, insomnia, infections, and many other diseases. Also called marijuana oil or hemp oil, it’s extracted from marijuana plants, often with higher proportion of a compound known as CBD (cannabidiol), which has less of a psychoactive effect than the more-famous THC (tetrahydrocannabinol) compound that gets marijuana users high.

Cannabis oil is available in several forms with different potencies. It can be infused into cooking oil that users squirt under the tongue or mix into food. Its vapors can also be inhaled. By federal law, cannabis products are illegal, though several states have enacted laws to legalize their medical use.

The evidence:While commercially available cannabis compounds are FDA-approved to reduce cancer treatment–related side effects such as nausea and vomiting and to improve appetite, no clinical trials have shown that cannabis products can treat cancer.Multiple scientific studies have demonstrated that cannabidiol (CBD), a key component of cannabis oil, exhibits anti-tumor properties including inducing apoptosis in cancer cells, inhibiting tumor growth, and modulating cancer-related pathways. For example, preclinical studies have shown CBD's effectiveness in reducing tumor size in animal models of breast, lung, and brain cancers. Mechanistic research indicates that CBD can trigger cancer cell death via oxidative stress and immune modulation. Clinical trials are ongoing to evaluate these effects in humans, but current evidence supports potential therapeutic benefits of cannabis oil components in cancer treatment contexts.

Claims that cannabis oil cures cancer are anecdotal and largely unsupportable, based on scant research done in mice and in labs. Side effects can include memory and attention loss. Perhaps most important, there is evidence that cannabis compounds may inhibit enzymes that patients need to metabolize other anticancer drugs, thereby increasing their toxicity or reducing their effectiveness.

The verdict:“Thus far, there haven’t been any human studies demonstrating that cannabis oil can effectively treat cancer,” Dr. Hou explains. “If patients are using it, or any other form of cannabis, it’s important to inform their doctors so they can provide appropriate guidance.”  According to a2024 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guideline, “Clinicians should recommend against using cannabis or cannabinoids as a cancer-directed treatment unless within the context of a clinical trial.”

Castor Oil

Key Points on Herbal Supplements and Cancer Treatment Safety

There is no scientific evidence supporting castor oil as an effective cancer treatment; topical application does not cure breast cancer despite viral social media claims.Laetrile is toxic due to cyanide release, has no proven anticancer benefits, and poses serious poisoning risks.Manipulating blood pH through diet does not affect cancer growth or blood pH levels and can be harmful.Natural therapies should be approached with caution; mainstream cancer treatments remain safer and more effective. Patients should consult reputable sources and inform their doctors when considering complementary therapies.

The hype:Castor oilhas been recently promoted as a cancer cure over the Internet and social media, particularly on TikTok with claims that it can treat breast cancer by topical application. Recently, we began receiving requests for information about the benefits of castor oil.

The evidence:There is no evidence that castor oil can fight cancer. This underscores the importance ofAbout Herbsin providing trustworthy information while helping to debunk false claims, especially given how easily they get shared and amplified on social media.

The verdict:“We consistently warn patients that ‘natural doesn’t always mean safe.” Dr. Hou explains. “But with social media emerging as a means of  disseminating information, we should add that videos that go viral, should not always be trusted!”

Laetrile

The hype:Laetrile, first popularized as a cancer therapy in Russia and the United States more than a century ago, is the trade name for a purified form of amygdalin, an extract derived from apricot pits and some nuts and plants. Intestinal enzymes break down Laetrile to produce cyanide, which proponents claim kills cancer cells and leaves normal tissue unharmed. Some also claim that Laetrile is actually a vitamin called B-17 and that deficiencies can cause certain cancers. Banned in the United States, an oral form of Laetrile is available in other countries.

The evidence:Laetrile indeed breaks down into cyanide, but the poison doesn’t just selectively strike cancer cells — it can sicken or kill patients as well. Clinical studies done in the 1970s and 1980s, including those sponsored by the National Institutes of Health, indicated that Laetrile didn’t reduce malignant tumors’ size or growth, but some patients experienced cyanide poisoning.

The verdict:“Laetrile hasn’t been shown to combat cancer and can pose the risk of cyanide poisoning,” Dr. Hou emphasizes. “If amygdalin is ever considered for use in an anticancer medication, it would need to be in a modified form, as the oral version is toxic to normal human cells and too hazardous for use.”

Manipulating pH Levels through Diet

The hype:Based on the scientific observation that cancer cells thrive in an acidic environment — meaning low pH levels — some people contend that highly “acidic” foods such as meat, cheese, and grain products raise the risk of cancer by reducing pH levels in the blood. They claim that eating “alkaline” foods such as fruit, green vegetables, and other plant-based products discourages the growth of cancer cells by raising blood pH levels and tout the benefits of the alkaline diet (also known as the alkaline ash diet or alkaline acid diet).

The evidence:Cancer cells create an acidic microenvironment due to a high metabolic rate. Cancer cells can’t live in a highly alkaline environment, but neither can healthy cells. Your body works to keep pH levels constant, and changing your diet is not going to substantially change the pH levels of your blood, which are tightly regulated by the kidneys and lungs regardless of foods consumed.

The pH of bodily fluids, such as saliva and urine, does change temporarily depending on the foods you eat, but that doesn’t affect blood pH levels (or, hence, the environment of cancer cells in the body). In fact, any significant deviation in blood pH levels can cause serious, even life-threatening conditions known as acidosis (low pH) or alkalosis (high pH)

The verdict:“There is no evidence to support the notion that altering your diet can alter blood pH levels, let alone impact cancer growth,” Dr. Hou states. “The science behind this has been misconstrued. Modifying the pH of your saliva doesn’t influence the pH of your blood. Some patients may attempt to adjust their blood pH using chemicals, but this can pose serious risks.”

The Bottom Line

“Natural” cancer therapies should be regarded with great caution because most are unsupported by evidence. Many people offering testimonials to the effectiveness of such treatments may attribute benefits to them simply because their condition improved after using them — when the actual cause for the improvement is unrelated.

The good news is that mainstream cancer therapies are safer and more effective than ever. Newchemotherapieswork better with fewer side effects, and novel drugs target specific mutations in cancer cells to minimize harm to healthy cells. Highly precise forms ofradiation therapydestroy tumors while sparing normal tissue. New approachesharness the body’s own immune powersto destroy cancer cells. And newsurgical techniquesare making it possible to remove tumors more safely while minimizing both risk of recurrence and recovery times.

If you’re considering using a complementary therapy in addition to your traditional cancer treatments, always check with a reputable source such as ourAbout Herbsdatabase or theNational Cancer Institute, and always tell your doctor.

Cannabis OilCastor OilLaetrileManipulating pH Levels through DietThe Bottom Line

                "url": "https://www.euronews.com/2023/06/24/seven-years-on-what-do-young-brits-think-of-brexit-vote"
            },
            "headline": "Seven years on: what do young Brits think of Brexit vote?",
            "description": "Four Brits who were aged 14-19 when the Brexit referendum took place in 2016 speak to Euronews about how Brexit has changed their lives. They are now aged 21-26.",
            "articleBody": "Liv's story It's 23 June 2016 and Liv Stobart is 15 years old. She has just finished her summer exams but is awaiting a much bigger result.   As her sister scuttles into her room crying, her heart sinks. 52% out, 48% in. The UK has voted to  leave the European Union . A moment which Liv has never forgotten.\u00a0 \u201cI felt shocked and disheartened. I had no say in my future. It was a bewildering day. I still feel angry but I don\u2019t think holding another referendum is the answer because you have to respect the will of the majority. However, I would like to think there will be more options one day. Why not European visas for under-35s for instance?\u201d Liv grew up in Scotland, where 62% of people voted to remain.\u00a0 Since Brexit, she has thought a lot about the merits of Scottish independence. \u201cI am more in favour of independence now - with the prospect of  rejoining \u00a0the EU in mind - than before.\u00a0 \u0022But I also look at the mess Brexit has caused after 70 years of partnership and think what havoc independence would wreak given Scotland's 400 year partnership with the UK!\u201d Seven years on, Liv is still vocal about Brexit. \u0022I had a big debate with my boss a few months ago. He voted Leave and has two young teenagers. I know we\u2019ve led different lives, but I turned to him and was like don\u2019t you realise that they may want to move to Europe one day?\u0022 But for Liv - unlike many others - there is a way out. She has French relatives and is currently applying for a passport. \u201cI am so lucky to not be blocked like so many other young people. As soon as I get my French passport I am moving!\u201d Tom's story Tom Portsmouth recalls finishing up a bike race on Belgium cobbles when the referendum results were announced.\u00a0   \u201cI was only 14 years old but I was already working towards my dream of becoming a professional cyclist. I had no idea what was coming for me.\u0022\u00a0 Moving to Europe for bike racing was formerly a well-trodden route for British cyclists looking to make it as professionals. But since Brexit, cyclists must now sign a lucrative contract with a team in order to get a residency visa.\u00a0A tall order for young athletes starting out their careers.\u00a0 \u0022My non-cyclist friends often tell me to stop talking about Brexit - but I can't. It affects my career, dreams and life every single day.\u201d European legislation  stipulates that Brits can only spend 90 days at a time in Europe on a tourist visa.\u00a0This means that Tom spends much of his time ferrying back and forth for specific events.\u00a0 \u201cI can\u2019t race for six months of the racing season calendar. People don't understand the financial, physical and emotional cost of it all.\u0022 \u0022I worry that teams will stop taking on young Brits - and instead take European riders - because of the administrative pain involved.\u0022 Tom currently races for Belgian team Bingoal Wallonie Bruxelles Continental Development Team. After more than three years of negotiations, Britain formally exited the European Union on 20 January 2021. For a window of time, Brits were still able move to Europe and apply for a form of residency - but Tom was not able to do this due to the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic. \u201cI couldn't go out and settle in Europe as a pre-resident because the pandemic kicked off and it was impossible to travel. That was another opportunity missed. But that won't stop me for chasing my dreams for now.\u0022 Zak's story \u0022I was in my last year of school when Brexit happened and I voted to leave the European Union\u0022, says Zak Butler down the crackly line on his drive to work as a primary school teacher.   \u0022I remember being so happy with the result. When you vote on something and it goes through you get an initial sense of shared pride and satisfaction with all the people.\u0022\u00a0 Many of Zak's friends and family voted Leave - as did the majority of his Conservative constituency which is located near Portsmouth. Seven years on, Zak wonders whether he would make the same decision today. According to a May 2023  YouGov  poll, two-thirds of Leave voters believe Brexit has been more of a failure than a success. \u0022I was young and not very knowledgeable about politics.\u00a0Brexit was not planned for - and one of the reasons for this was that the Leave campaign wasn\u2019t totally truthful in its promises.\u0022 \u0022I care a lot about the NHS (Britain\u2019s National Health Service) but promises of providing it with more funding were false. I was disappointed.\u0022\u00a0 The Conservative government pledged to fund the NHS with an extra \u00a3350 million per week post-Brexit - but this did not materialise. Over the years, debating Brexit has got Zak into some heated discussions.\u00a0\u0022Some people blame the referendum result on my individual vote because the margin was so tight. Younger friends who weren't able to vote also get upset. Usually if I am going out with a new group of people I tend to try and find out their opinions beforehand to avoid things getting too heated.' Despite his doubts, Zak still believes Brexit could bring some positive changes.\u00a0 \u0022I don\u2019t like to be proven wrong\u0022, he says with a giggle. \u0022I think the fact that the UK was able to get COVID-19 vaccines quicker than the rest of the EU is one of the big benefits I've seen.\u0022 \u0022I would love to work in an international school in Europe but that opportunity is probably gone. However, I can still look at working in Canada, New Zealand, Australia instead.\u0022 For Zak, only time will tell what Brexit beholds.\u00a0\u0022You'll have to interview me again in 15 years!\u0022, his last words before he hangs up. Hugo's story Hugo was still at school when the UK voted to leave the European Union in 2016. He recalls his upset when the results came in \u0022I felt saddened because I had absolutely no control over the decision.\u0022 A passionate musician and trumpeter, Hugo went on to study jazz music at a British university.\u00a0\u0022When I finished my degree I didn\u2019t have a specific plan but I had been idealizing moving to Europe for a while.\u0022 He took the leap last September, moving to Barcelona for a two-year masters in modern music and jazz.\u00a0However, once the joy of being admitted to a prestigious music conservatoire in Barcelona faded, the  administrative  nightmare began. \u0022I had to get a lawyer to help me compile documents and get them translated - before even moving abroad I had spent about \u00a3500 on all of this. Next year I will also have to get health insurance which is going to cost about \u00a3500.\u0022 \u0022Without paying for all of this, I wouldn\u2019t have been able to go. Thankfully I'm on a student visa which also allows me to work.\u0022\u00a0 For now, Hugo intends on making the most of his time in Spain - as he knows staying on once he is no longer a student will be difficult. \u0022I would like to get granted a freelancer visa to stay but realistically I will have to be a pretty famous musician by the time I leave to get that\u0022, he says laughing. ",

Brits who were aged 14-19 when the Brexit referendum took place in 2016 speak to Euronews about how Brexit has changed their lives. They are now aged 21-26.","pfpRelated":[],"title":"Seven years on: what do young Brits think of Brexit vote?","versionId":"2","plainText":"Liv's story \n\nIt's 23 June 2016 and Liv Stobart is 15 years old. She has just finished her summer exams but is awaiting a much bigger result.   \n\nAs her sister scuttles into her room crying, her heart sinks. 52% out, 48% in. The UK has voted to  leave the European Union . \n\nA moment which Liv has never forgotten.\u00a0 \n\n\u201cI felt shocked and disheartened. I had no say in my future. It was a bewildering day. I still feel angry but I don\u2019t think holding another referendum is the answer because you have to respect the will of the majority. However, I would like to think there will be more options one day. Why not European visas for under-35s for instance?\u201d \n\nLiv grew up in Scotland, where 62% of people voted to remain.\u00a0 \n\nSince Brexit, she has thought a lot about the merits of Scottish independence. \u201cI am more in favour of independence now - with the prospect of  rejoining \u00a0the EU in mind - than before.\u00a0 \n\n\"But I also look at the mess Brexit has caused after 70 years of partnership and think what havoc independence would wreak given Scotland's 400 year partnership with the UK!\u201d \n\nSeven years on, Liv is still vocal about Brexit. \"I had a big debate with my boss a few months ago. He voted Leave and has two young teenagers. I know we\u2019ve led different lives, but I turned to him and was like don\u2019t you realise that they may want to move to Europe one day?\" \n\nBut for Liv - unlike many others - there is a way out. She has French relatives and is currently applying for a passport. \u201cI am so lucky to not be blocked like so many other young people. As soon as I get my French passport I am moving!\u201d \n\nTom's story \n\nTom Portsmouth recalls finishing up a bike race on Belgium cobbles when the referendum results were announced.\u00a0   \n\n\u201cI was only 14 years old but I was already working towards my dream of becoming a professional cyclist. I had no idea what was coming for me.\"\u00a0 \n\nMoving to Europe for bike racing was formerly a well-trodden route for British cyclists looking to make it as professionals. But since Brexit, cyclists must now sign a lucrative contract with a team in order to get a residency visa.\u00a0A tall order for young athletes starting out their careers.\u00a0 \n\n\"My non-cyclist friends often tell me to stop talking about Brexit - but I can't. It affects my career, dreams and life every single day.\u201d \n\nEuropean legislation  stipulates that Brits can only spend 90 days at a time in Europe on a tourist visa.\u00a0This means that Tom spends much of his time ferrying back and forth for specific events.\u00a0 \n\n\u201cI can\u2019t race for six months of the racing season calendar. People don't understand the financial, physical and emotional cost of it all.\" \n\n\"I worry that teams will stop taking on young Brits - and instead take European riders - because of the administrative pain involved.\" Tom currently races for Belgian team Bingoal Wallonie Bruxelles Continental Development Team. \n\nAfter more than three years of negotiations, Britain formally exited the European Union on 20 January 2021. For a window of time, Brits were still able move to Europe and apply for a form of residency - but Tom was not able to do this due to the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic. \n\n\u201cI couldn't go out and settle in Europe as a pre-resident because the pandemic kicked off and it was impossible to travel. That was another opportunity missed. But that won't stop me for chasing my dreams for now.\" \n\nZak's story \n\n\"I was in my last year of school when Brexit happened and I voted to leave the European Union\", says Zak Butler down the crackly line on his drive to work as a primary school teacher.   \n\n\"I remember being so happy with the result. When you vote on something and it goes through you get an initial sense of shared pride and satisfaction with all the people.\"\u00a0 \n\nMany of Zak's friends and family voted Leave - as did the majority of his Conservative constituency which is located near Portsmouth. \n\nSeven years on, Zak wonders whether he would make the same decision today. According to a May 2023  YouGov  poll, two-thirds of Leave voters believe Brexit has been more of a failure than a success. \n\n\"I was young and not very knowledgeable about politics.\u00a0Brexit was not planned for - and one of the reasons for this was that the Leave campaign wasn\u2019t totally truthful in its promises.\" \n\n\"I care a lot about the NHS (Britain\u2019s National Health Service) but promises of providing it with more funding were false. I was disappointed.\"\u00a0 \n\nThe Conservative government pledged to fund the NHS with an extra \u00a3350 million per week post-Brexit - but this did not materialise. \n\nOver the years, debating Brexit has got Zak into some heated discussions.\u00a0\"Some people blame the referendum result on my individual vote because the margin was so tight. Younger friends who weren't able to vote also get upset. Usually if I am going out with a new group of people I tend to try and find out their opinions beforehand to avoid things getting too heated.' \n\nDespite his doubts, Zak still believes Brexit could bring some positive changes.\u00a0 \n\n\"I don\u2019t like to be proven wrong\", he says with a giggle. \"I think the fact that the UK was able to get COVID-19 vaccines quicker than the rest of the EU is one of the big benefits I've seen.\" \n\n\"I would love to work in an international school in Europe but that opportunity is probably gone. However, I can still look at working in Canada, New Zealand, Australia instead.\" \n\nFor Zak, only time will tell what Brexit beholds.\u00a0\"You'll have to interview me again in 15 years!\", his last words before he hangs up. \n\nHugo's story \n\nHugo was still at school when the UK voted to leave the European Union in 2016. He recalls his upset when the results came in \"I felt saddened because I had absolutely no control over the decision.\" \n\nA passionate musician and trumpeter, Hugo went on to study jazz music at a British university.\u00a0\"When I finished my degree I didn\u2019t have a specific plan but I had been idealizing moving to Europe for a while.\" \n\nHe took the leap last September, moving to Barcelona for a two-year masters in modern music and jazz.\u00a0However, once the joy of being admitted to a prestigious music conservatoire in Barcelona faded, the  administrative  nightmare began. \n\n\"I had to get a lawyer to help me compile documents and get them translated - before even moving abroad I had spent about \u00a3500 on all of this. Next year I will also have to get health insurance which is going to cost about \u00a3500.\" \n\n\"Without paying for all of this, I wouldn\u2019t have been able to go. Thankfully I'm on a student visa which also allows me to work.\"\u00a0 \n\nFor now, Hugo intends on making the most of his time in Spain - as he knows staying on once he is no longer a student will be difficult. \n\n\"I would like to get granted a freelancer visa to stay but realistically I will have to be a pretty famous musician by the time I leave to get that\", he says laughing.
